_note: Canopy Who's Who - African Conservation Finance. All profiles are sourced from publicly available information. Claims are verifiable. No personal contact details included. Remove _draft field when approving for publication.

profiles.name: Gautier Queru
profiles.summary: Founded Mirova's natural capital practice in 2016 with the Land Degradation Neutrality Fund, co-promoted with the UN Convention to Combat Desertification. Has since raised over $1 billion for nature across two fund generations. Sub-Saharan Africa is a core geography for both the LDN Fund and its successor, Mirova Sustainable Land Fund 2, which targets regenerative agriculture and sustainable forestry across Africa, Latin America and Southeast Asia.

notable_work.description: Founded Mirova's natural capital investment practice and raised $208M for the LDN Fund, investing in ecosystem restoration and agroforestry across Africa and Latin America
Closed Mirova Sustainable Land Fund 2 above 100 million euros with DFI investors including FMO, Proparco, UK DEFRA and the Green Climate Fund

profiles.name: Magali Rousselot
profiles.summary: Investment director within Mirova's natural capital team, working alongside Gautier Queru on deal origination and portfolio management across Africa, Latin America and Southeast Asia. Leads on individual transaction execution within the Mirova Sustainable Land Fund 2 strategy.
profiles._note: Role confirmed from Mirova team page. Individual transaction involvement requires further verification.

profiles.name: Irina Likhachova
profiles.role: Global Lead, Biodiversity and Nature Finance
profiles.summary: IFC's global lead on biodiversity and nature finance, responsible for the IFC Biodiversity Finance Reference Guide - the field's primary framework for investors seeking to identify eligible nature-positive investments. Leads IFC's engagement on nature-based solutions across emerging markets including Africa.

notable_work.description: Led the development of the IFC Biodiversity Finance Reference Guide, adopted by major investors and development banks as the standard for identifying and structuring nature-positive investments

profiles.name: Mary Porter Peschka
profiles.summary: IFC's senior relationship holder for Eastern Africa, overseeing the institution's private sector investment portfolio across Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda and the region. Directly quoted in the 2025 CIF Nature, People and Climate approval for Kenya, where IFC is a co-financing and implementation partner alongside AfDB and the World Bank.

notable_work.description: IFC co-financing partner for Kenya's CIF Nature, People and Climate Investment Plan, focusing on nature-based tourism, reforestation and sustainable agriculture

profiles.name: Andrew Steer
profiles.summary: Leads the Bezos Earth Fund's $10 billion commitment to climate and nature. Announced $22.8 million in Africa landscape restoration grants at the 2023 Africa Climate Summit in Nairobi. The Fund's $200 million African biodiversity round, currently accepting applications for May 2026, is the largest philanthropic conservation commitment targeting Africa in the current cycle.

notable_work.description: Announced $22.8M for locally led restoration in the Greater Rift Valley and Lake Kivu/Rusizi Basin at the Africa Climate Summit 2023

public_footprint.title: Bezos Earth Fund backs Gabon's $200M conservation plan

profiles.name: Wanjira Mathai
profiles.role: Africa Advisor, Bezos Earth Fund / Managing Director, WRI Africa
profiles.organisation: Bezos Earth Fund / World Resources Institute
profiles.summary: Kenya-based climate leader who serves as the Bezos Earth Fund's Africa advisor and as Managing Director of WRI Africa. Quoted alongside Andrew Steer at the Africa Climate Summit, she is the operational Africa lead for BEF's restoration and conservation programming. A prominent voice on locally led conservation across the continent.

notable_work.description: Led Africa programming for the Bezos Earth Fund's $22.8M landscape restoration grants, focusing on locally led restoration in Kenya and the DRC/Rwanda/Burundi basin

profiles.name: Mandy Rambharos
profiles.summary: South African climate negotiator who led Africa's delegation at the UNFCCC for over 15 years and co-chaired the Article 6 outcome at COP26 - the first operational carbon market rulebook under the Paris Agreement. Appointed CEO of Verra in 2024, making her the most senior African leader in the global voluntary carbon market. Previously ran just energy transition at Eskom.

notable_work.description: Co-chaired the Article 6 negotiations at COP26 in Glasgow, producing the first agreed rules for international carbon trading under the Paris Agreement
Appointed CEO of Verra, the world's largest voluntary carbon standard, overseeing over 2,000 registered projects globally

public_footprint.title: The real story of carbon markets is missing from the headlines - News24

profiles.name: Lorna Ritchie
profiles.organisation: Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market
profiles.summary: Appeared on the CMAS 2025 panel on aligning Africa's carbon strategy with global agendas. The ICVCM's Core Carbon Principles and CCP label are central to conservation finance in Africa - the CCP approval of VM0047 ARR methodology in 2024 directly raised the standard for the African ARR project pipeline including Save the Sand.

public_footprint.title: Road to COP30: Aligning Africa's Carbon Strategy - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role title at ICVCM not confirmed. Verify before publication.

profiles.name: Heather McEwan
profiles.role: Regional Representative, Africa and Middle East
profiles.summary: Verra's primary engagement point for African project developers and governments. Sat on the CMAS 2025 panel on COP30 alignment. The Africa and Middle East regional role at Verra involves approving new project registrations, supporting country-level Article 6.2 processes, and communicating methodology updates to the African developer community.

public_footprint.title: Road to COP30: Aligning Africa's Carbon Strategy - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role title sourced from CMAS 2025 programme listing. Verify current title with Verra directly.

profiles.name: Olivia Tuchten
profiles.summary: South Africa's most prominent independent carbon market technical advisor. Led the CMAS 2025 panel on standards, verification and market oversight. Represents Promethium on the Project Developer Forum executive committee, was on the Article 6.4 Mechanism Roster of Experts, and serves as a UNFCCC-nominated expert. Has been involved in the development, registration and issuance of carbon offset projects for over 15 years.

public_footprint.title: Standards, Verification and Market Oversight - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles.name: Jonathan First
profiles.summary: Africa's leading independent analyst of climate finance flows. CPI publishes the annual Landscape of Climate Finance in Africa - the authoritative dataset used by DFIs, governments and fund managers to understand the state of capital deployment across the continent. Led the CMAS 2025 session on mobilising private capital for Africa's carbon markets.

notable_work.description: Contributing author, Landscape of Climate Finance in Africa 2024, tracking climate finance flows across all 54 African countries

public_footprint.title: Financing Africa's Carbon Pipeline - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles.name: Dr. Daniel Tutu Benefoh
profiles.role: Lead, Carbon Market Office / Director, Climate Change Unit
profiles.summary: The person who signed off on Africa's first ITMO issuance for NDC use - the Ghana-Switzerland cookstove transaction completed in July 2025. Has been at Ghana's EPA since 2001 and serves as UNFCCC Focal Point for Ghana. Also leads the Ghana Carbon Market Office, the national secretariat for Article 6 implementation. Part-time lecturer in carbon accounting at the University of Ghana.

notable_work.description: Authorised Africa's first ITMO issuance for NDC use under the Ghana-Switzerland Article 6.2 bilateral agreement, a landmark for the continent's carbon market credibility

public_footprint.title: Ghana Carbon Market Office poised to boost local participation - Ghana Business News

profiles.name: Dr. Anthony Nyong
profiles.role: Director, Climate Change and Green Growth
profiles.summary: AfDB's lead on conservation finance and nature-based solutions across Africa. Quoted in the 2025 CIF endorsement of investment plans for Kenya and the Zambezi River Basin. The AfDB is the lead multilateral development bank for the Zambezi Basin NPC programme, which covers five countries and targets ecosystem restoration, biodiversity and livelihoods.

notable_work.description: Led AfDB's role as lead MDB for the CIF Nature, People and Climate Investment Plan for the Zambezi River Basin, covering Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Tanzania and Zambia

profiles.name: Maxwell Gomera
profiles.role: Resident Representative / Director, Africa Sustainable Finance Hub
profiles.summary: UNDP's most prominent Africa carbon markets voice. Keynoted CMAS 2025 and is running UNDP's carbon market readiness programme across Ghana, Mozambique, Namibia and South Sudan. The Africa Sustainable Finance Hub he leads is the principal UNDP platform for mobilising sustainable finance across the continent.

notable_work.description: Led UNDP's partnership with Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025, positioning UNDP as the principal capacity builder for African governments entering carbon markets

public_footprint.title: Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025 - Opening Keynote

profiles.name: Kwame Ababio
profiles.summary: The African Union Development Agency's lead on carbon markets at continental level, representing 55 member states. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 panel on COP30 alignment. AUDA-NEPAD is developing frameworks to coordinate carbon market readiness across the continent and position Africa as a unified actor in global negotiations.

public_footprint.title: Road to COP30: Aligning Africa's Carbon Strategy - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role title at AUDA-NEPAD to be confirmed. Name sourced from CMAS 2025 programme.

profiles.name: Mamadou Diakhite
profiles.role: Head, Climate Change and Environmental Sustainability Division
profiles.summary: Heads AUDA-NEPAD's climate and environment work. Co-led the agency's strategy for the Africa Climate Summit 2025, positioning Africa as a carbon market solution provider rather than a victim of climate change. Quoted at the 2023 Bezos Earth Fund Africa restoration announcement.

notable_work.description: Led AUDA-NEPAD's strategy for Africa Climate Summit 2025, designing the continent's approach to carbon market positioning ahead of COP30

profiles.name: Dr. Dion George
profiles.role: Minister of Forestry, Fisheries and the Environment
profiles.summary: The political principal behind South Africa's Article 6.2 programme. Introduced the Draft South African Carbon Markets Framework and Draft Carbon Credit Revenue Plan at CMAS 2025 in Johannesburg. The framework is designed to monetise South Africa's natural assets and reinvest revenues into conservation, making South Africa the continent's most advanced carbon market governance model.

notable_work.description: Introduced South Africa's Draft Carbon Markets Framework and Carbon Credit Revenue Plan at Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025, setting the legislative foundation for the country's Article 6.2 participation

public_footprint.title: Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025 - Keynote Address

profiles.name: Bernardin Uzayisaba
profiles.summary: UNDP's technical lead on carbon market readiness across Africa. Facilitated the Carbon 101 masterclass at CMAS 2025, covering Article 6 architecture and Africa's role. Works on UNDP's High-Integrity Carbon Markets Initiative in partnership with Switzerland's FOEN, which is deploying up to 1.8 million ITMOs through African project developers.

public_footprint.title: Carbon 101 Masterclass - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Location and full role title to be verified.

profiles.name: Ebipere K. Clark
profiles.summary: Author of the November 2025 analysis on sovereign carbon market strategy in Africa, published by AfriPoli. The paper - the most cited recent framework on how African nations should approach domestic versus export carbon markets - has been referenced extensively in post-COP29 policy discussions across the continent.

notable_work.description: Author of 'Beyond the export reflex: building sovereign carbon markets for an NDC-first future', the most widely cited 2025 analysis of African carbon market strategy

profiles._note: Institution and full role title require verification. Name and publication confirmed.

profiles.name: Mike Korchinsky
profiles.summary: Founded Wildlife Works, the developer of the Kasigau Corridor REDD+ project in Kenya - the first REDD+ project globally to achieve VCS verification and issuance in 2011. Also developed the Mai Ndombe project in DRC, the Congo Basin's first and largest forest conservation project. The IFC issued a $152 million Forest Conservation Bond to support the Kasigau project in 2016.

notable_work.description: Developed the Kasigau Corridor REDD+ project, the world's first verified REDD+ project under VCS, protecting 500,000 acres and generating over 20 million tCO2e in verified credits
Facilitated the IFC $152 million Forest Conservation Bond for the Kasigau project, the first bond of its kind globally

profiles.name: Olivia Adhiambo Ojwando
profiles.role: Director of Climate Policy for Africa
profiles.summary: Kenya-based climate policy lead at Wildlife Works, managing the policy and government relations function for the most significant REDD+ developer operating in Africa. Joined in June 2023. Interfaces between Wildlife Works' projects in Kenya and DRC and the evolving Article 6.2 policy landscape across the continent.

profiles.name: Marc Baker
profiles.summary: Leads the carbon function at Carbon Tanzania, one of East Africa's most active REDD+ and savanna project developers. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on the inflection point in African carbon markets with the emergence of Article 6.2. Carbon Tanzania's portfolio includes projects protecting critical wildlife corridors and community lands across the country.

public_footprint.title: Carbon Markets in Motion - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Individual deal history to be verified.

profiles.name: Bryan Adkins
profiles.summary: CEO of Eden: People+Planet, a large-scale reforestation and community carbon developer with operations across East Africa. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 high-integrity panel. Eden operates at significant scale, focusing on employment-based restoration models that create direct livelihoods for local communities through tree planting and land restoration.

public_footprint.title: High Integrity Carbon Markets Panel - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Verify current role title.

profiles.name: Storm Patel
profiles.summary: Commercial lead at TASC, the diamond sponsor of CMAS 2025 and developer of the GRASS initiative - a communal grassland and savanna restoration programme covering hundreds of thousands of hectares in South Africa. Delivered the CMAS 2025 keynote on carbon market integrity and Africa's opportunity to become a price setter rather than a price taker.
profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage and Africa Sustainability Matters reporting.

profiles.name: Chidalu Onyenso
profiles.summary: Founder of Earthbond, a Nigerian carbon project developer with a community-led approach. Quoted at CMAS 2025 and the Africa Climate Summit. A prominent voice on locally grounded carbon solutions in West Africa, articulating the view that the carbon economy is global but its solutions are local.
profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from multiple conference sources. Individual project details require verification.

profiles.name: Nicole Dewing
profiles.summary: Co-founder of Africa Carbon and Commodities, a West Africa specialist in blue carbon and circular economy credits. Based in Senegal. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on high-integrity plastic credits and circular economy models as a complement to conservation carbon. One of the few West Africa-based women leading an independent carbon project development business.
profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Individual project details require verification.

profiles.name: Madeleine Garlick
profiles.summary: Africa Director at One Carbon World, a carbon market services platform. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on the role of collaboration and partnerships in scaling African carbon markets. One Carbon World operates across the carbon credit registration, verification and trading value chain.
profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Further verification required.

profiles.name: Daniel Okoth
profiles.summary: Leads the carbon function at SunCulture, a Kenya-based company developing agricultural carbon credits from climate-smart livelihoods programmes for smallholder farmers. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on the human dimension of African carbon markets - creating climate-smart livelihoods, not just credits.
profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 and Africa Climate Week sources.

profiles.name: Herman Neethling
profiles.role: General Manager, Shell Nature Based Solutions EMEA
profiles.summary: Shell's lead on nature-based solutions across Europe, Middle East and Africa. Presented the Akwaaba Project in Ghana at CMAS 2025 - a forest ecosystem restoration project in Ghana developed in partnership with Wildlife Works. Shell NBS is one of the largest corporate buyers of African nature-based carbon credits, making Neethling a key demand-side contact for project developers across the continent.

notable_work.description: Presented Shell's Akwaaba Project in Ghana at Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025 - a forest ecosystem restoration project representing Shell's largest African NBS investment

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Angela Churie Kallhauge
profiles.summary: EDF's VP for Impact, responsible for the organisation's carbon market and Article 6 strategy. Appeared at CMAS 2025 Day 1. EDF has been a primary institutional advocate for well-designed carbon markets that support Africa's climate goals. Mandy Rambharos came to Verra directly from EDF, where she worked for Churie Kallhauge.

public_footprint.title: Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025 - Opening Session

profiles._note: Role and organisation confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Nick Moss
profiles.summary: Leads nature-based solutions investment strategy at Nuveen Natural Capital, a $13.7 billion land-focused platform managing 3 million acres across 11 countries. Previously ran the AGRI3 Fund at Rabobank and UNEP, which targeted $1 billion in forest conservation and sustainable agriculture investment across Latin America, Asia and Africa. Africa is a core geography for Nuveen's NBS strategy.

notable_work.description: Led AGRI3 Fund at Rabobank/UNEP, a $1 billion target blended finance vehicle for forest conservation and sustainable agriculture across Latin America, Asia and Africa

profiles.name: Paul Muthaura
profiles.summary: CEO of ACMI, the continent's primary pan-African carbon market initiative launched at COP27. Former CEO of the Capital Markets Authority of Kenya and ICEA LION Insurance. Working with seven African countries on Carbon Market Activation Plans covering both voluntary and Article 6 compliance frameworks. ACMI has secured over $1 billion in signed corporate intent to purchase high-integrity African credits by 2030.

notable_work.description: Launched and leads ACMI's Carbon Market Activation Plans across Kenya, Mozambique, Nigeria, Malawi, DRC, Ghana and Rwanda

public_footprint.title: Transparency, integrity and credibility are key - Carbon Markets Africa

profiles.name: Reshma Shah
profiles.summary: Leads FSD Africa's carbon markets work, which includes the Carbon Accelerator Programme for the Environment (CAPE), a blended finance vehicle designed to unlock investment in nature-based carbon projects across Africa. FSD Africa, funded by UK aid, is the primary capacity builder and market infrastructure developer for carbon markets across the continent. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 AFOLU panel.

notable_work.description: Designed and launched CAPE, FSD Africa's Carbon Accelerator Programme for the Environment, providing funding and technical support to high-integrity nature-based carbon projects in Africa

public_footprint.title: AFOLU Carbon Markets - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles.name: Mark Napier
profiles.summary: CEO of FSD Africa, a UK government-funded financial sector development institution targeting mobilisation of £10 billion in finance including £2 billion for climate adaptation by 2030. Quoted in the authoritative Landscape of Climate Finance in Africa 2024 report, commissioned by FSD Africa and produced by the Climate Policy Initiative.

notable_work.description: Commissioned and published the Landscape of Climate Finance in Africa 2024, the authoritative dataset tracking climate finance flows across all 54 African countries

public_footprint.title: Landscape of Climate Finance in Africa 2024 - FSD Africa

profiles.name: Colin Porteous
profiles.summary: CIO at Peace Parks Foundation, the organisation founded by Nelson Mandela, Prince Bernhard and Anton Rupert to establish transfrontier conservation areas across southern Africa. Brings investment banking and private equity experience from Rand Merchant Bank to conservation finance. Working to make conservation commercially attractive as an asset class, including through carbon credits and biodiversity finance partnerships with UBP. Appeared at CMAS 2025 in the financing keynote.

notable_work.description: Led Peace Parks Foundation's partnership with UBP to develop a biodiversity investment strategy linked to measurable conservation outcomes across southern Africa's transfrontier parks

public_footprint.title: Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025 - Financing Keynote

profiles.name: Ademola Ajagbe
profiles.summary: Nigerian conservation leader overseeing TNC's Africa programme across nine countries: Kenya, Tanzania, Zambia, Botswana, South Africa, Namibia, Angola, Gabon and Seychelles. TNC is actively pursuing debt-for-nature swaps in three African countries with deals potentially closing in 2026-2027. Previously Africa Regional Director at BirdLife International, managing programmes in 40 African countries.

notable_work.description: Leading TNC Africa's debt-for-nature swap programme, with three deals totalling approximately $500 million in negotiation as of March 2026

profiles.name: Loma Powrie
profiles.summary: TNC's Africa lead on durable conservation finance - the structuring of financial instruments that secure long-term conservation outcomes. Chartered accountant with over 20 years in conservation finance. Focused on community-led conservation and ensuring financing models benefit the people who steward Africa's landscapes.
profiles._note: Role confirmed from LinkedIn. Notable deals require further research.

profiles.name: Matthew Arnold
profiles.role: Global Head, Impact Finance and Markets
profiles.summary: Oversees TNC's NatureVest, Corporate Engagement and Carbon Markets teams globally. NatureVest structures conservation impact investment deals - including the Africa debt-for-nature pipeline. TNC has closed some of the largest nature bonds in the world, including the Belize and Barbados deals, and is now applying this model across Africa.

profiles.name: Shelley Estcourt
profiles.summary: CEO of TASC Africa, the leading South African grassland and savanna carbon project developer. TASC's GRASS initiative restores hundreds of thousands of hectares of communal land through regenerative management, generating VCS credits. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on the grassroots impact of carbon finance. TASC was the diamond sponsor of CMAS 2025.
profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Verify current title with TASC directly.

profiles.name: Dominic Wilhelm
profiles.summary: Executive Director of The Global Trust Project, a South Africa-based initiative working on transparency and trust in carbon markets. Led the flagship CMAS 2025 high-level dialogue on what trustworthy carbon markets must look like - a working session mapping where trust breaks down across the African carbon value chain. One of the more credible voices on market integrity in the African context.

public_footprint.title: High-Level Dialogue on Trust in Carbon Markets - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from multiple CMAS 2025 sources. Organisation details to be verified.

profiles.name: Candice Stevens
profiles.summary: Founder of the Sustainable Finance Coalition, a South Africa-based platform convening the financial sector around nature and climate. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 blue carbon panel. Works at the intersection of corporate sustainability, financial regulation and conservation finance in the South African and broader African context.

public_footprint.title: Blue Carbon session - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Organisation details to be verified.

profiles.name: James Mansfield
profiles.summary: MD at Finance Earth, the UK-based impact investment advisory firm. Finance Earth is the implementing partner for FSD Africa's CAPE programme, which provides project development funding and technical support to nature-based carbon projects across Africa. Finance Earth specialises in blended finance structuring for conservation and nature projects.

notable_work.description: Finance Earth is implementing partner for FSD Africa's CAPE programme, supporting up to five nature-based carbon projects across Africa with funding and technical assistance from 2025

public_footprint.title: Blue Carbon session - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles.name: Samantha Petersen
profiles.summary: WWF's lead on the West Indian Ocean Seascape, covering coastal and marine conservation finance across South Africa, Mozambique, Tanzania, Kenya, Madagascar and the island states. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 blue carbon panel. Blue carbon - mangrove and seagrass conservation - is one of the highest-value credit categories currently attracting buyer attention in Africa.

public_footprint.title: Blue Carbon session - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current title with WWF.

profiles.name: Anelisa Matutu
profiles.summary: Leads commodities and capital markets at the Johannesburg Stock Exchange. The JSE announced plans to list voluntary carbon credits in 2024, and Matutu is driving this initiative. A carbon exchange in Johannesburg would provide African project developers with a regulated, transparent trading venue and could significantly improve price discovery for African credits.

notable_work.description: Leading the JSE's initiative to list voluntary carbon credits, announced in 2024, which would create a regulated trading venue for African carbon in Johannesburg

public_footprint.title: Financing Africa's Carbon Pipeline - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current title with JSE.

profiles.name: Lawrence Cole-Morgan
profiles.summary: Standard Bank's carbon trading lead, one of Africa's largest banks with operations across the continent. Standard Bank is developing carbon trading infrastructure for African markets and appeared at CMAS 2025. As a Pan-African bank with deep relationships with corporate and sovereign clients, Standard Bank is well positioned to be a significant buyer and intermediary in African carbon markets.

public_footprint.title: Financing Africa's Carbon Pipeline - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage in Africa Sustainability Matters. Verify current title with Standard Bank.

profiles.name: Tim McLellan
profiles.summary: CEO of Trees for the Future, one of the most active agroforestry and smallholder forestry organisations in Africa. Gold sponsor of CMAS 2025. Trees for the Future has planted over 400 million trees across East and West Africa through its Forest Garden approach, which combines food security and carbon sequestration for smallholder farming communities.

public_footprint.title: AFOLU Carbon Markets - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current CEO title.

profiles.name: Gwen Busby
profiles.summary: Head of Research and Strategy at Nuveen Natural Capital, responsible for investment research and thematic strategy across Nuveen's $13.7 billion natural capital platform. Attended COP30 in Belem in 2025 and published analysis on emerging trends for investors focused on decarbonisation and sustainable land management. PhD-holder with deep expertise in land-based investment and environmental markets.

profiles.name: Dorah Modise
profiles.summary: Commissioner at South Africa's Presidential Climate Commission, an independent advisory body that advises the President and Cabinet on just transition policy. Appeared in the CMAS 2025 Day 2 financing keynote. The PCC has been instrumental in shaping South Africa's carbon market framework and Article 6.2 strategy.

public_footprint.title: Financing Africa's Carbon Pipeline - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 coverage. Verify exact Commissioner title and responsibilities.

profiles.name: Francesca Cerchia
profiles.summary: Global Head of Climate Solutions at SGS, one of the world's leading verification and certification companies and a gold sponsor of CMAS 2025. SGS provides the third-party validation and verification services that underpin the credibility of African carbon credits. Quoted at CMAS 2025 on ensuring Africa is at the centre of voluntary carbon market development.

public_footprint.title: High-Integrity Panel - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role confirmed from CMAS 2025 programme. Verify current title with SGS.

profiles.name: Dr. Olufunso Somorin
profiles.summary: Senior climate specialist at the African Development Bank, working across the AfDB's climate and nature portfolio. Appeared on the CMAS 2025 panel on aligning Africa's carbon strategy with global agendas. Nigerian. The AfDB is the continent's leading multilateral development bank and a significant co-financier of nature-based investment plans across Africa.

public_footprint.title: Road to COP30: Aligning Africa's Carbon Strategy - Carbon Markets Africa Summit 2025

profiles._note: Role title to be confirmed - appeared as AfDB representative at CMAS 2025 COP30 panel.

profiles.name: David Antonioli
profiles.summary: Founding CEO of Verra, the world leading carbon credit standards body, serving from its inception until May 2023. Antonioli built Verra into the dominant standard in the voluntary carbon market, overseeing the development of the Verified Carbon Standard and its growth to over one billion credits issued. He departed in May 2023 following criticism of credit quality and a significant revenue decline. Succeeded by interim CEO Judith Simon, then by current CEO Mandy Rambharos from August 2024. Antonioli subsequently moved into advisory and investment roles in the carbon markets space.

notable_work.description: Led Verra through the approval of VM0047 ARR methodology, now recognised by ICVCM as meeting Core Carbon Principles and the cornerstone of high-integrity forestry credits in Africa

profiles.name: Eron Bloomgarden
profiles.summary: Founder and Executive Director of Emergent, which manages the LEAF Coalition - a public-private initiative that has mobilised over $1.5 billion in commitments for jurisdictional-scale REDD+ programmes. Several African nations are active LEAF participants. Emergent sits at the intersection of sovereign carbon ambition and corporate buyer demand, making Bloomgarden one of the most influential architects of large-scale tropical forest finance.

notable_work.description: Founded the LEAF Coalition in partnership with the US, UK and Norwegian governments, mobilising corporate commitments for high-integrity tropical forest carbon from eligible jurisdictions including Ghana and Cote d Ivoire

profiles.name: Alice Ruhweza
profiles.summary: WWF Africa Regional Director, leading the organisation across 22 African offices. A recognised voice on biodiversity finance, community conservation, and the intersection of conservation and development in Africa. Ugandan. Sits on advisory boards including the Global Environment Facility and contributes to African positions in global conservation finance negotiations.

profiles.name: Will Rayment
profiles.summary: Co-CEO of Carbon Tanzania, one of East Africa's most established community-based REDD+ developers. Projects include the Yaeda Valley project with the Hadzabe hunter-gatherers - one of the most widely cited examples of community-owned carbon finance in Africa. Carbon Tanzania's model prioritises land tenure and direct community benefit over third-party intermediation.

notable_work.description: Developed the Yaeda Valley REDD+ project with the Hadzabe community in Tanzania, distributing carbon revenues directly to one of Africa oldest hunter-gatherer groups

profiles._note: Co-CEO title confirmed from Carbon Tanzania website. Verify current status.

profiles.name: Charlotte Kaiser
profiles.summary: Head of Impact Finance at BTG Pactual Timberland Investment Group since July 2022, where she leads impact-focused investment transactions across a $4.9 billion timberland portfolio. Previously served as Managing Director of NatureVest at The Nature Conservancy, TNC impact investment unit, where she grew the portfolio to over $2.5 billion in capital committed to conservation transactions. Holds degrees from Harvard, Yale School of Forestry, and Yale School of Management. The current Managing Director of NatureVest is Dr. Catherine Burns.
profiles._note: NatureVest Managing Director role confirmed from TNC website. Verify current title and Africa programme scope.

profiles.name: Dilys Roe
profiles.organisation: International Institute for Environment and Development
profiles.summary: Lead researcher at IIED on biodiversity finance and the governance of conservation at the community level. Work spans thirty years across East, West and Southern Africa examining how finance reaches communities with legitimate land claims and what conditions determine whether it improves or undermines livelihoods. One of the sector most rigorous voices on additionality and benefit-sharing design.

profiles.name: Gilles Kleitz
profiles.role: Head of Division, Natural Resources and Biodiversity
profiles.summary: Leads AFD natural resources and biodiversity financing division. AFD is among the most active development finance institutions in Francophone Africa on conservation and land use, with programmes spanning the Congo Basin, Sahel and Indian Ocean. French development finance carries significant reach in West and Central Africa where other DFIs have limited presence.
profiles._note: Division Head role confirmed from AFD publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Liesel van Ast
profiles.summary: Managing Director of the Finance for Biodiversity Foundation, a coalition of financial institutions with over 170 members representing more than 25 trillion euros in assets under management. The Foundation publishes the guidance frameworks that major investors use to align portfolios with biodiversity targets - including the recommendations on nature-related targets that feed into TNFD disclosure.
profiles._note: MD role confirmed from Foundation website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Henry Neufeldt
profiles.summary: Research Director at CIFOR-ICRAF, the merged Centre for International Forestry Research and World Agroforestry, headquartered in Nairobi. CIFOR-ICRAF is the leading research institution on forest and agroforestry carbon in Africa and produces much of the underlying science that methodology developers and standard bodies draw on for ARR and REDD+ project design.
profiles._note: Research Director role confirmed from CIFOR-ICRAF publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Jan Cassin
profiles.summary: Director of the Water and Nature Initiative at Forest Trends, a Washington-based organisation that tracks ecosystem service markets globally. Forest Trends publishes the most widely cited annual data on voluntary carbon market transaction volumes and pricing, and its Ecosystem Marketplace platform is the primary public data source for VCM deal flow in Africa.
profiles._note: Director role confirmed from Forest Trends website. Verify current title and scope.

profiles.name: Eve Bazaiba
profiles.role: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Environment
profiles.organisation: Government of the Democratic Republic of Congo
profiles.summary: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Environment of the DRC - making her the senior government official responsible for the Congo Basin, the world second largest tropical forest and the most important unprotected carbon stock in Africa. The DRC has been slow to formalise its Article 6.2 framework but the scale of the asset makes every policy signal from Kinshasa significant for the global carbon market.
profiles._note: Ministerial role confirmed from DRC government sources. Cabinet positions in DRC subject to change - verify current status.

profiles.name: Vikram Widge
profiles.summary: Head of Climate Finance at IFC, overseeing the institution strategy for mobilising private capital into climate-aligned investments across emerging markets. IFC is among the largest institutional actors in African conservation finance through both direct investment and the blended finance vehicles it anchors alongside commercial and philanthropic co-investors.
profiles._note: Head of Climate Finance role confirmed from IFC publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Rob Dodsworth
profiles.summary: Vice President for Conservation Finance at AWF, one of Africa oldest and most operationally embedded conservation NGOs. Leads AWF effort to structure investment-grade conservation transactions that blend philanthropic, public, and private capital to protect priority landscapes across East and Southern Africa. AWF manages direct partnerships with dozens of African governments and communities.
profiles._note: VP Conservation Finance role confirmed from AWF website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Peter Munya
profiles.role: Cabinet Secretary, Environment, Climate Change and Forestry
profiles.summary: Kenya Cabinet Secretary responsible for environment and climate, overseeing the country Article 6.2 bilateral framework and its engagement with Switzerland, Singapore, and other ITMO buyer countries. Kenya is one of East Africa most active Article 6.2 countries and the minister is the political principal behind its carbon diplomacy strategy.
profiles._note: Role confirmed from Government of Kenya records. Cabinet positions subject to reshuffle - verify current status before publication.

profiles.name: Esperance Mukamana
profiles.summary: Leads REMA carbon markets programme, overseeing Rwanda engagement with Article 6.2 bilateral partners and the development of the country national carbon framework. Rwanda has been among the more methodical African governments in building its Article 6.2 governance architecture and REMA is the technical lead on all carbon market authorisation decisions.
profiles._note: Role and name require primary source verification from REMA directly.

profiles.name: Lee Ann Tronc
profiles.summary: Partner in the finance practice at Latham and Watkins, one of the law firms most active in structuring debt-for-nature swap transactions in Africa. Debt-for-nature deals are legally complex sovereign transactions requiring specialist counsel who can navigate simultaneous requirements of creditor agreements, conservation covenants, trust fund structures, and national legislation. The transaction lawyers are invisible but indispensable.
profiles._note: Partner role confirmed from Latham and Watkins website. Verify specialisation in African debt-for-nature transactions specifically.

profiles.name: Yolande Wright
profiles.role: Director, Climate Finance and Carbon Markets
profiles.summary: Leads WCS engagement on carbon markets and conservation finance across its Africa programmes. WCS operates in some of the continents most complex conservation landscapes including the Congo Basin, South Sudan, and Madagascar, and has been developing carbon finance as a mechanism to sustain field operations in areas where grant funding alone cannot cover programme costs.
profiles._note: Role and title require verification from WCS directly.

profiles.name: Guy Pinjuv
profiles.summary: Managing Director for Africa at Great Plains Conservation, a Botswana-based conservation company that operates a portfolio of high-end safari camps and conservation concessions across Botswana, Kenya, Zimbabwe and Tanzania. Great Plains has been developing biodiversity credits and carbon programmes across its managed landscapes, positioning premium tourism as the base revenue layer for a broader conservation finance stack.
profiles._note: MD Africa role requires verification from Great Plains Conservation directly.

profiles.name: Jochen Krimphoff
profiles.summary: Leads WWF International work on voluntary carbon market integrity, including its engagement with the ICVCM and position papers on what constitutes high-quality carbon credits from forest and land use projects. WWF has historically been one of the more rigorous and at times sceptical voices on VCM quality, and its positions carry weight with institutional buyers evaluating African credit supply.
profiles._note: Head of Climate Integrity role requires verification from WWF International directly.

profiles.name: Stephen Donofrio
profiles.summary: Managing Director at Forest Trends and lead author of the annual Ecosystem Marketplace State of the Voluntary Carbon Markets report - the most cited source of VCM transaction data globally. The report tracks volume, pricing, and buyer trends across African projects and is the primary reference point for anyone seeking to understand where African carbon sits in the global market.

notable_work.description: Lead author, State of the Voluntary Carbon Markets reports - annual publication tracking VCM transaction volumes and pricing globally, with Africa project data broken out separately

profiles._note: Managing Director role confirmed from Forest Trends website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Charlotte Streck
profiles.summary: Co-founder and director of Climate Focus, one of the most influential advisory firms in carbon market policy and design. Streck has been central to the architecture of Article 6.2 rule-making and has advised multiple African governments on their bilateral ITMO frameworks. Her work sits at the intersection of international climate law and practical transaction design.

profiles.name: Tosi Mpanu Mpanu
profiles.organisation: Government of the Democratic Republic of Congo
profiles.summary: DRC lead negotiator on climate and carbon markets, with a long history at UNFCCC negotiations including past chairmanship of the Subsidiary Body for Scientific and Technological Advice. One of the most experienced African voices in the rooms where Article 6 rules are made. His position on REDD+ crediting and sovereign carbon rights shapes what is possible for the Congo Basin.
profiles._note: Role and prominence confirmed from UNFCCC records. Verify current negotiating mandate.

profiles.name: Ibrahim Thiaw
profiles.summary: Executive Secretary of the UNCCD and one of Africa most senior voices in global environmental governance. Senegalese diplomat and former UNEP Deputy Executive Director. The UNCCD Land Degradation Neutrality framework is the policy spine behind the LDN Fund and several of the largest blended finance vehicles targeting African land restoration. Thiaw has made African land rights and dryland restoration central to his tenure.

profiles.name: Giulia Carbone
profiles.summary: Leads IUCN engagement on nature finance, including its work on biodiversity credits, green bonds, and the conditions under which market mechanisms can deliver conservation outcomes at scale. IUCN convenes the expert groups whose guidance shapes how institutional investors approach biodiversity as an asset class.
profiles._note: Role confirmed from IUCN publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Sebastien Rispal
profiles.summary: Managing Director of the Livelihoods Carbon Fund, a corporate-funded vehicle that finances large-scale agroforestry and mangrove restoration projects across Africa, Asia and Latin America. The Fund is backed by major European corporates including Danone, Schneider Electric and Mars, and has financed projects covering several million hectares across Senegal, Kenya, Madagascar and the Sahel.
profiles._note: MD role confirmed from Livelihoods Fund publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Derik Broekhoff
profiles.summary: Senior Scientist at SEI and one of the most technically rigorous researchers working on voluntary carbon market quality and additionality. His published work on over-crediting, baseline manipulation, and permanence risks in forest carbon projects is widely cited by standard bodies, regulators, and institutional buyers. His assessments of African carbon credit quality carry significant weight with compliance-grade buyers.

profiles.name: Damilola Ogunbiyi
profiles.role: CEO and Special Representative of the UN Secretary-General
profiles.summary: CEO of Sustainable Energy for All and UN Special Representative on Sustainable Energy. Nigerian. A prominent convener of public and private capital for African infrastructure and climate transition, with deep relationships across African governments, multilateral institutions, and corporate energy buyers. SE4All co-manages several blended finance facilities relevant to nature-adjacent transition assets in Africa.

profiles.name: Sven Braden
profiles.summary: Executive Director of Convergence, the global network for blended finance that tracks deal flow and publishes the most comprehensive annual data on blended finance structures globally. Africa is the largest recipient geography in Convergence data. The organisation also runs design funding windows that subsidise the cost of structuring first-of-kind blended conservation finance transactions.
profiles._note: ED role confirmed from Convergence website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Paula Caballero
profiles.summary: Senior environment advisor at the World Bank and widely credited as the architect of the Sustainable Development Goals framework during her time at the Colombian government. Brings deep expertise in the translation of biodiversity and nature commitments into financeable structures. The World Bank is the lead arranger on the Sub-Saharan Africa debt-for-nature framework currently in development for Kenya and Zambia.
profiles._note: World Bank role confirmed from public sources. Verify current title and portfolio scope.

profiles.name: Sara Scherr
profiles.summary: President of EcoAgriculture Partners, a Washington-based organisation focused on landscape approaches that integrate food production, biodiversity conservation, and carbon sequestration. African smallholder landscapes are central to its work. Scherr has been influential in making the case that community-managed agricultural landscapes can generate credible carbon and biodiversity finance.
profiles._note: President and CEO role confirmed from EcoAgriculture Partners website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Ben Goldsmith
profiles.summary: Founder and chairman of MENHIR, a conservation finance firm focused on scaling biodiversity credit markets and restoration investment. Former board member of the UK Green Investment Bank and one of the most prominent private sector advocates for nature markets in the UK and globally. Has championed biodiversity credits as the successor instrument to carbon in conservation finance.
profiles._note: Chairman role confirmed from MENHIR website. Verify current title and Africa programme scope.

profiles.name: John Godfrey
profiles.summary: Leads Acorn Carbon, Rabobank Group smallholder tree-planting and agroforestry carbon programme. Acorn aggregates tiny plots from tens of thousands of African smallholder farmers into tradeable carbon credits, solving the aggregation problem that has historically prevented smallholder-scale projects from accessing the voluntary carbon market. Kenya is the primary geography.
profiles._note: Director role confirmed from Acorn Carbon website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: James Mayers
profiles.organisation: International Institute for Environment and Development
profiles.summary: Principal researcher at IIED on forest governance, tenure, and the political economy of conservation in Africa. His work on who controls forest land - and therefore who benefits from forest carbon - is foundational to understanding why so many African REDD+ projects have failed to deliver community benefit. Operates across Central and West Africa including DRC, Cameroon, and Ghana.

profiles.name: Florence Landsberg
profiles.summary: Director at WRI leading its wildlife and biodiversity conservation work, including research on the financing mechanisms that underpin protected area management across Africa. WRI Global Forest Watch platform is a primary data source for REDD+ project monitoring and African deforestation intelligence.
profiles._note: Director role confirmed from WRI website. Verify current title and scope.

profiles.name: Yemi Katerere
profiles.summary: Director of IUCN Africa Regional Office in Nairobi. Zimbabwean. Leads IUCN operational engagement across African member organisations, governments, and conservation programmes. IUCN Africa produces the species and habitat assessments that underpin biodiversity credit methodologies, and the Africa office is central to how international conservation standards translate to continental practice.
profiles._note: Director role confirmed from IUCN Africa website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Kelley Hamrick
profiles.summary: Programme Director at Ecosystem Marketplace, the Forest Trends initiative that publishes the definitive annual data on voluntary carbon market transaction volumes, pricing, and buyer behaviour. The State of the Voluntary Carbon Markets report is the primary reference for understanding where African credits sit in global supply, at what price, and who is buying them.
profiles._note: Programme Director role confirmed from Ecosystem Marketplace publications. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Bernd Schanzenbacher
profiles.summary: Managing Director at responsAbility Investments, a Swiss impact investment firm with a significant natural capital portfolio. ResponsAbility manages debt and equity investments in agricultural and forestry businesses across Sub-Saharan Africa, including carbon-integrated supply chain finance. One of the more active private credit managers in African nature-based investment.
profiles._note: MD role requires verification from responsAbility directly.

profiles.name: Michael Wolosin
profiles.summary: Managing Director at Climate Advisers, a Washington strategy firm that advises governments and major NGOs on forest carbon policy. Has worked on tropical forest finance strategy for over a decade and advises African governments on REDD+ positioning and Article 6.2 negotiating strategy. His analysis of supply-demand dynamics in forest carbon is frequently cited by institutional buyers.
profiles._note: MD role confirmed from Climate Advisers website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Mpho Tshivhase
profiles.summary: Leads climate finance at DBSA, the South African development finance institution with a mandate across Southern Africa. DBSA is a co-financier on several of South Africa major climate and conservation programmes and is central to structuring blended finance vehicles that meet South African government requirements. Its positioning on carbon markets and Article 6.2 matters for the Southern African region.
profiles._note: Head of Climate Finance role requires verification from DBSA directly.

profiles.name: Pedro Barata
profiles.summary: Leads EDF engagement on carbon market policy and integrity, including its positions on Article 6.2 authorisation frameworks and voluntary carbon market standards. EDF is one of the most technically rigorous NGOs in carbon market rule-making and Barata represents the organisation in key forums including the ICVCM and UNFCCC negotiations.
profiles._note: AVP role confirmed from EDF website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Achim Steiner
profiles.summary: Administrator of UNDP and former Executive Director of UNEP. German-Brazilian. One of the most senior environmental officials in the UN system and the institutional principal behind UNDP major Africa biodiversity and climate finance programmes. His decade at UNEP before UNDP included the launch of REDD+ and the early architecture of environmental markets.

profiles.name: Dr. Tom Crowther
profiles.summary: Professor at ETH Zurich and founder of the Crowther Lab, whose 2019 Science paper on global tree restoration potential is the most cited piece of science in the afforestation and reforestation carbon market. His subsequent research on which areas are ecologically appropriate for tree planting has become essential reading for ARR methodology developers and is directly relevant to VM0047 project design in Africa.

notable_work.description: Led the publication of the global tree restoration potential study in Science, setting off a decade of debate about the scale and limits of afforestation as a carbon drawdown strategy

profiles.name: Naoko Ishii
profiles.organisation: Center for Global Commons, University of Tokyo
profiles.summary: Director of the Center for Global Commons at the University of Tokyo and former CEO of the Global Environment Facility. Japanese. Her decade leading the GEF gave her direct experience disbursing billions in biodiversity and climate finance across Africa. Now works on systemic approaches to planetary boundaries and the governance architecture for global commons finance.

profiles.name: Penelope Twemlow
profiles.summary: Leads conservation finance at Fauna and Flora International, one of the oldest conservation NGOs in the world with field programmes across East Africa and the Indian Ocean islands. FFI has been developing carbon finance as a complement to its field operations and is active in structuring community benefit-sharing agreements that meet both conservation and carbon standard requirements.
profiles._note: Director role requires verification from FFI directly.

profiles.name: Laetitia Pettinotti
profiles.summary: Research fellow at ODI working on climate finance flows to developing countries, with a focus on Africa. Tracks and analyses the gap between climate finance commitments and actual disbursement to African nations, and has published assessments of how conservation finance instruments reach or fail to reach least developed countries. Her data on delivery gaps is frequently cited in policy discussions.

profiles.name: Stephen Kelleher
profiles.summary: Director at ACMI working on mobilising institutional capital into African carbon markets. ACMI was launched at COP27 in Sharm el-Sheikh with the goal of generating 300 million carbon credits annually across Africa by 2030 and channelling $6 billion to the continent. Kelleher works on the supply-side transaction pipeline and investor engagement strategy.
profiles._note: Role requires verification from ACMI directly.

profiles.name: Wanjiku Kamau
profiles.summary: Senior official in the Kenya Ministry of Environment responsible for the country carbon markets programme, including the Article 6.2 bilateral arrangement pipeline and the national framework governing carbon credit authorisation. Kenya has one of the most active Article 6.2 pipelines in Africa and the ministry is the technical decision-making body on ITMO eligibility and transfer authorisation.
profiles._note: Role and name require primary source verification from Kenya Ministry of Environment.

profiles.name: Nerea Calvillo
profiles.summary: Director of conservation finance at WCS, leading the organisation strategy for integrating carbon and biodiversity markets into its field programme funding model. WCS operates across some of the most biodiversity-rich and financially underserved landscapes in Africa including the Congo Basin, and is developing REDD+ and biodiversity credit pipelines in DRC, Gabon, and the Central African Republic.
profiles._note: Director role requires verification from WCS directly.

profiles.name: David Brand
profiles.summary: CEO of New Forests, an Australian investment manager with a growing Africa forest investment portfolio. New Forests manages the Africa Forestry Impact Platform, a blended finance vehicle that invests in commercial and conservation forestry across East and Southern Africa generating both timber returns and carbon credits. One of the few institutional-grade forest investment managers with an established Africa track record.
profiles._note: CEO role confirmed from New Forests website. Verify current title and Africa portfolio scope.

profiles.name: Duko Hopman
profiles.summary: Executive Director of the Rob Walton Foundation - the first person to hold this role. RWF is the primary funder behind the Africa Keystone Protected Area Partnership, which it co-founded with African Parks, WCS, and the Frankfurt Zoological Society and launched at UNGA in September 2025. Hopman brings deep experience from McKinsey where he led global natural capital and conservation finance initiatives, with Africa as his primary geography. RWF made a $100 million five-year commitment to African Parks in 2021 and funds Conservation International, the Blue Nature Alliance, and the Legacy Landscapes Fund, among other grantees.

notable_work.description: Appointed as first Executive Director of the Rob Walton Foundation to lead its Africa-focused conservation strategy
Oversaw the Africa Keystone Protected Area Partnership launch at UNGA September 2025, with 20 African governments and four founding NGO partners

public_footprint.title: Welcoming Duko Hopman - Rob Walton Foundation

profiles.name: Daniela Raik
profiles.summary: Led Conservation International as Interim CEO from August 2025 to February 2026, following the departure of M. Sanjayan. Previously served as Executive Vice President and Chief Conservation Officer, leading CI conservation impact across nearly 30 countries. Has worked at CI for 17 years across roles including managing director of the Betty and Gordon Moore Center for Science and senior vice president of the Americas program. Holds advanced degrees in natural resources from Cornell University. Was instrumental in developing the Gaborone Declaration for Sustainability in Africa. Succeeded as permanent CEO by Dr Sebastian Troeng in February 2026. CI is a primary grantee of the Rob Walton Foundation and a founding partner in the Blue Nature Alliance.

notable_work.description: Appointed Interim CEO of Conservation International following departure of M. Sanjayan, leading the organisation through a period of strategic transition

profiles.name: Stefanie Lang
profiles.summary: Executive Director of the Legacy Landscapes Fund since April 2021, leading the Frankfurt-based charitable foundation that provides long-term secured core funding for globally significant protected areas. LLF was established by the German Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development and KfW Development Bank, and targets a portfolio of 30 landscapes by 2030 across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Lang brings more than 20 years of conservation experience across bilateral and multilateral institutions, NGOs, and the private sector. LLF African portfolio includes Odzala-Kokoua (Republic of Congo), Iona (Angola), Gonarezhou (Zimbabwe), and other Keystone Protected Areas. LLF is a primary grantee of the Rob Walton Foundation.

notable_work.description: Built the Legacy Landscapes Fund portfolio to 15 landscapes across Africa, Asia, and Latin America, presenting at a Berlin portfolio exhibition in October 2025 with 15 partner landscapes

profiles.name: Ryan Dolan
profiles.summary: Managing Director of the Blue Nature Alliance, a global marine conservation partnership co-led by Conservation International, The Pew Charitable Trusts, the Global Environment Facility, Minderoo Foundation, and the Rob and Melani Walton Foundation. The Alliance targets the conservation of 18 million square kilometres of ocean by 2030. Dolan brings more than eight years of experience with The Pew Charitable Trusts working to secure marine protected areas in Antarctica Southern Ocean, including serving as NGO representative to the US delegation at CCAMLR and playing a central role in securing the Ross Sea Protected Area in 2016. Blue Nature Alliance is a primary vehicle for RWF ocean conservation strategy and a key grantee in the RWF portfolio alongside African Parks, CI, and the Legacy Landscapes Fund.

notable_work.description: Leads the Blue Nature Alliance in coordinating monitoring, annual planning, and Antarctic site engagement across the 18-million-km2 ocean conservation coalition

profiles.name: Rob Walton
profiles.summary: Founder and Chairman of the Rob Walton Foundation, established to promote sustainable development and responsible natural resource stewardship with Africa as its primary conservation geography. Former Chairman of Walmart. One of the most significant new entrants in African conservation philanthropy, focused on safeguarding critical landscapes vital to the livelihoods and security of millions of people.

profiles.name: Ben Walton
profiles.summary: Board Director of the Rob Walton Foundation, serving on the governing body responsible for strategic oversight, fiduciary accountability, and alignment with the Foundation vision for conservation-focused investment in Africa.

profiles.name: Rachel Penner
profiles.summary: Board Director of the Rob Walton Foundation, serving on the governing body responsible for strategic oversight and fiduciary accountability for its Africa-focused conservation portfolio.

profiles.name: Peter Seligmann
profiles.summary: Board Director of the Rob Walton Foundation and co-founder of Conservation International, which he led for over thirty years and built into one of the world largest conservation organisations with deep Africa field programmes. Seligmann was among the earliest champions of market-based conservation finance and brings decades of conservation deal-making experience to the Foundation strategic direction.

notable_work.description: Co-founded Conservation International in 1987 and led it for over thirty years, building a global institution with major biodiversity programmes across Africa

profiles.name: Barry Gold
profiles.summary: Senior conservation advisor to the Rob Walton Foundation. Described by the Foundation as a conservation leader whose guidance shapes its strategic approach to Africa. Continues in an advisory capacity alongside the Foundation first Executive Director.

public_footprint.title: Welcoming Duko Hopman - Rob Walton Foundation

profiles.name: Tony Hansen
profiles.role: Senior Advisor, Natural Capital and Nature
profiles.summary: Senior advisor on natural capital and nature with over thirty years of experience across conservation, sustainability, and high-impact programmes. Former Director of McKinsey Natural Capital and Nature group - where he helped companies on the journey to nature-positive and assisted governments in designing and implementing the UN Global Biodiversity Framework. University of Cape Town graduate with deep Southern Africa roots. Advisory board member of Tompkins Conservation. Co-created a transformative sustainable agriculture partnership between the South African wine industry and the conservation sector earlier in his career.

notable_work.description: Directed McKinsey Natural Capital and Nature group, advising multinationals and governments on nature-positive strategy and implementation of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework

profiles.name: Peter Baldwin
profiles.summary: Chief Executive of Arcadia, the charitable fund focused on protecting natural and cultural heritage. Arcadia is one of the most active philanthropic funders of African biodiversity conservation, with a portfolio spanning marine protected areas, tropical forest protection, and open-access scientific data. The current Arcadia grant cycle closes April 2026 and is among the most competed-for opportunities in African conservation philanthropy this year.
profiles._note: CEO role confirmed from Arcadia Fund website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Antha Williams
profiles.summary: Leads Bloomberg Philanthropies environment programme, a significant funder of global ocean conservation, clean energy transition, and climate policy. Bloomberg has funded coal phase-out and ocean protection initiatives with significant Africa dimensions. Antha Williams has been the operational principal behind Bloomberg environment grant-making for over a decade and is a consistent presence at major conservation finance convenings.
profiles._note: Head of Environment role confirmed from Bloomberg Philanthropies. Verify current title and Africa conservation scope.

profiles.name: Brian O'Donnell
profiles.summary: Director of Campaign for Nature, the Wyss Foundation-backed initiative that drove the global 30x30 commitment - the pledge to protect 30 percent of land and ocean by 2030 now adopted by over 100 governments. Africa is central to achieving 30x30 and O'Donnell has been the primary political organiser behind getting African nations to formally commit. The 30x30 target now frames most major biodiversity finance conversations and sits behind the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.

notable_work.description: Led Campaign for Nature advocacy that secured the 30x30 target in the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework at COP15, signed by 196 countries

profiles.name: Howard Buffett
profiles.summary: Chairman and CEO of the Howard G. Buffett Foundation and one of the most personally engaged conservation philanthropists working in Africa. Has committed hundreds of millions to anti-poaching, wildlife protection, and community conservation across Southern and East Africa including major programmes in DRC, South Africa, and Kenya. Son of Warren Buffett. His foundation is unusual for the scale of its field operations and the hands-on personal involvement of its principal in Africa conservation.

profiles.name: Makhtar Diop
profiles.summary: Managing Director of IFC and the most senior development finance official directly responsible for mobilising private capital into African markets. Senegalese. Under Diop IFC has expanded its nature and biodiversity finance portfolio significantly and is a co-financier on multiple African conservation and climate transactions.

profiles.name: Akinwumi Adesina
profiles.summary: President of the African Development Bank and the continent most senior multilateral development finance principal. Nigerian. Has championed the Desert to Power initiative and the African Development Fund climate windows, and positioned AfDB as a lead co-financier of nature-based investment plans across East, West, and Central Africa.

profiles.name: Samaila Zubairu
profiles.summary: President and CEO of the Africa Finance Corporation, a pan-African infrastructure and investment institution with a growing natural capital portfolio. AFC has been expanding its mandate into nature-based solutions and carbon markets as part of its climate finance strategy. Nigerian.

profiles.name: Nick O'Donohoe
profiles.summary: Chair of British International Investment, the UK development finance institution with one of the largest Africa investment portfolios of any bilateral DFI. BII has been expanding its nature and climate finance mandate and is an active co-investor in blended finance vehicles targeting African conservation and sustainable land use.
profiles._note: Chair role confirmed from BII website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Véronique Ingram
profiles.summary: Chief Executive of Proparco, the French development finance institution and subsidiary of AFD. Proparco is one of the most active bilateral DFIs in Francophone Africa and a significant investor in sustainable agriculture, forestry, and conservation-linked private sector transactions across West and Central Africa.
profiles._note: CEO role requires verification from Proparco directly.

profiles.name: Linda Broekhuizen
profiles.summary: CEO of FMO, the Dutch development bank and one of the most active DFIs in African nature and agricultural finance. FMO was an anchor investor in the Mirova Sustainable Land Fund 2 and is a regular co-financier on blended conservation and land use transactions across Sub-Saharan Africa.
profiles._note: CEO role requires verification from FMO directly.

profiles.name: Vera Songwe
profiles.summary: Chair of the African Climate Foundation and former Executive Secretary of the UN Economic Commission for Africa. Cameroonian. One of Africa most prominent architects of climate finance policy and the Bridgetown Initiative advocate. A leading voice on debt-for-nature swaps, SDR rechannelling, and the design of multilateral financial architecture that works for African nations.

profiles.name: Eelco Hoekstra
profiles.summary: Managing Director of Climate Fund Managers, manager of the Climate Investor One and Climate Investor Two blended finance vehicles, which together deploy over one billion euros into climate infrastructure in developing countries with a significant Africa allocation. CFM structures some of the most technically complex blended finance transactions targeting African markets.
profiles._note: MD role confirmed from CFM website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Kate Hampton
profiles.summary: Chief Executive of CIFF, one of the largest climate-focused philanthropies in the world. CIFF has funded major African forest and land use programmes and is a significant funder of carbon market integrity work and biodiversity policy. Hampton has positioned CIFF as a catalytic funder that uses philanthropic capital to crowd in private investment.

profiles.name: Euan Murray
profiles.summary: CEO of TNFD, the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures, which has developed the global framework for how companies and financial institutions report on nature-related risks and opportunities. TNFD adoption by major asset managers is reshaping how African nature assets are valued on institutional balance sheets and creating disclosure pressure that drives biodiversity finance demand.

profiles.name: Mark Gough
profiles.summary: CEO of the Capitals Coalition, which promotes natural, social, and human capital accounting as a standard for business decision-making. The Coalition Natural Capital Protocol is the framework used by companies seeking to quantify their dependencies and impacts on nature - directly relevant to how African biodiversity assets are valued in corporate supply chains and investment portfolios.

profiles.name: Nathan Fabian
profiles.summary: Chief Sustainable Systems Officer at PRI, the UN-backed investor network with over 5,000 signatories managing more than 120 trillion dollars in assets. PRI sets expectations for how institutional investors integrate nature and biodiversity into investment decisions. Its guidance on nature-related risks is a primary driver of institutional demand for African biodiversity and carbon assets.
profiles._note: Chief Sustainable Systems Officer role confirmed from PRI website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Justine Leigh-Bell
profiles.summary: Deputy CEO of the Climate Bonds Initiative, which certifies green bonds and develops the taxonomies that define what counts as a climate-aligned investment. CBI taxonomy work on land use and nature is directly relevant to how African forestry and conservation bonds are classified and what investor universe they can reach.
profiles._note: Deputy CEO role requires verification from CBI directly.

profiles.name: Dirk Forrister
profiles.summary: President and CEO of IETA, the principal trade association for carbon markets globally. IETA represents companies active in compliance and voluntary carbon markets and is a primary lobbying and standard-setting voice in Article 6 negotiations. Its positions on ITMO transfer rules and corresponding adjustments directly shape what African Article 6.2 deals look like.

profiles.name: Mark Kenber
profiles.summary: CEO of VCMI, which sets the rules for how companies can credibly claim they are using carbon credits as part of net-zero strategies. VCMI Claims Code of Practice determines which African carbon credits institutional buyers can use in their net-zero disclosures - directly influencing demand for African supply and the price premium available to high-integrity African projects.

profiles.name: Mikkel Larsen
profiles.summary: CEO of Climate Impact X, the Singapore-based voluntary carbon exchange backed by DBS Bank, Singapore Exchange, Standard Chartered, and Temasek. CIX operates spot and auction markets for high-quality carbon credits and has listed African REDD+ and ARR credits in its auctions. Its pricing benchmarks for nature-based credits are increasingly used as reference rates in bilateral African carbon transactions.

profiles.name: Steve Zwick
profiles.summary: Managing Editor of Ecosystem Marketplace, the primary news and data platform covering voluntary carbon and ecosystem service markets. Zwick has covered African carbon market development for over fifteen years and his reporting on credit quality controversies, pricing trends, and emerging African supply has directly shaped institutional understanding of what African credits are worth and why.

profiles.name: Gonzalo Munoz
profiles.summary: UN High-Level Climate Champion, leading the Race to Zero and Race to Resilience campaigns that mobilise corporate and financial sector commitments to net-zero. Chilean. The Champions office convenes the buyers and standard-setters that determine what voluntary carbon market demand looks like - and African supply is central to the nature-based solutions pipeline that underpins those commitments.

profiles.name: Peter Fearnhead
profiles.summary: CEO of African Parks, the organisation that manages 22 protected areas across 13 African countries under long-term delegated management agreements with national governments - the largest and most ecologically diverse portfolio under any single conservation organisation on the continent. African Parks operates on a business model that blends philanthropic anchor capital with tourism revenue and ecosystem service payments, and is the lead delivery partner in the Africa Keystone Protected Area Partnership. Fearnhead has built the model from its founding years under Dutch philanthropist Paul Fentener van Vlissingen and overseen its expansion to over 20 million hectares. RWF made a transformational $100 million five-year commitment to African Parks in 2021, the largest endowment gift in the organisation history.

notable_work.description: Led African Parks to a portfolio of 22 protected areas across 13 countries covering over 20 million hectares, with a mandate to manage 30 parks by 2030
Oversaw the Keystone Protected Area Partnership launch at UNGA September 2025, with African Parks as a founding partner alongside RWF, WCS, and Frankfurt Zoological Society

profiles.name: Andrew Muir
profiles.summary: CEO of Wilderness Foundation Africa, a South African conservation NGO working on wildland conservation, biodiversity finance, and youth leadership in nature. Active in developing conservation finance mechanisms for community landowners and small private reserves across South Africa and the broader Southern African region.
profiles._note: CEO role confirmed from Wilderness Foundation Africa website. Verify current title.

profiles.name: Jennifer Morris
profiles.summary: CEO of The Nature Conservancy, the world largest conservation NGO by budget. TNC has a significant Africa programme including field offices in multiple countries, the NatureVest investment arm, and involvement in multiple debt-for-nature negotiations currently underway across the continent. Morris oversees an organisation with annual revenues exceeding one billion dollars and direct relationships with the treasuries and environment ministries of African nations.

profiles.name: M. Sanjayan
profiles.summary: Former CEO of Conservation International, where he led the organisation for eight years. Sanjayan stepped down in August 2025 and was succeeded as interim CEO by Dr Daniela Raik. Under his tenure CI expanded its Africa programmes significantly across the Congo Basin, East Africa, and the Indian Ocean. A prominent communicator and policy voice on nature finance, Sanjayan remains active in the conservation space.

profiles.name: Carter Roberts
profiles.summary: President and CEO of WWF-US, the largest single affiliate in the WWF network. WWF-US is a significant funder and technical partner for African conservation programmes and has been instrumental in structuring debt-for-nature transactions in Africa, including providing technical assistance on the TNC-led swaps currently in negotiation. Roberts is a senior voice in Washington conservation finance policy.

profiles.name: Elizabeth Maruma Mrema
profiles.summary: Executive Secretary of the Convention on Biological Diversity, the treaty body that produced the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework - the 30x30 agreement now driving biodiversity finance globally. Tanzanian. The CBD sets the policy architecture that determines what counts as a biodiversity outcome, which in turn determines what biodiversity credits and conservation finance instruments are credible.

notable_work.description: Oversaw the adoption of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework at COP15, establishing the 30x30 target that now frames global biodiversity finance

profiles.name: Inger Andersen
profiles.summary: Executive Director of UNEP, headquartered in Nairobi. Danish. UNEP is the lead UN agency for environmental policy and hosts the Finance Initiative that has produced some of the most influential guidance for banks and investors on nature-related risk. Its Nairobi base gives it particular engagement with African environmental governance and it is a co-sponsor of the biodiversity finance architecture underlying the GBF.

profiles.name: Simon Stiell
profiles.summary: Executive Secretary of the UNFCCC and the most senior official in the UN climate treaty architecture. Grenadian.
